applications: entra.html – Financial Portal with Microsoft Entra ID authentication and client‑side RBAC
okta.html – Financial Portal with Okta authentication and client‑side RBAC
public.html – Unauthenticated version of the portal exposing all sections

authentication.method: MSAL.js (loginPopup) using Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) – OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect
authentication.details: clientId and authority are hard‑coded; token is stored in sessionStorage; silent token acquisition on page load.

authentication.method: Okta Auth JS – redirect flow (signInWithRedirect) using OpenID Connect
authentication.details: clientId, issuer and redirectUri are hard‑coded; authentication state is kept by Okta SDK; no explicit token handling in the page.

authorization.implementation: Roles are read from the id token claim `roles`. UI sections (dashboard, reports, admin) are shown/hidden via DOM manipulation based on role (Admin, Analyst, User).
Roles are inferred from Okta group membership (`user.groups`). UI sections are shown/hidden in the same way as Entra.

authorization.type: No authorization – all sections are visible.

roles: Admin – full access to dashboard, reports, and admin sections
Analyst – access to dashboard and reports
risks: Client‑side RBAC can be bypassed by manipulating the DOM or crafting requests directly to backend APIs (if any).
Sensitive financial data is rendered in the browser before any server‑side authorization check.
Tokens and user data are stored in `sessionStorage`, which is vulnerable to XSS attacks.
Hard‑coded client IDs, tenant IDs, and redirect URIs expose configuration details and may be misused.
No HTTPS enforcement shown; if served over HTTP, tokens can be intercepted.
No validation of token signatures or expiration on the client; expired or tampered tokens could be accepted.
Public version (`public.html`) leaks full admin UI without any protection, providing an easy target for information disclosure.
Lack of CSRF protection for logout actions that rely on simple redirects.
recommendations: Move RBAC enforcement to a trusted backend service; the client should only request data it is authorized to receive.
Validate JWT signatures, issuer, audience, and expiration on the server before using any claims.
Store tokens in secure, httpOnly cookies rather than `sessionStorage` to mitigate XSS.
Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) and sanitize any dynamic content to reduce XSS risk.
Do not hard‑code tenant IDs, client IDs, or redirect URIs in public‑facing code; load them from environment variables or a secure configuration service.
Serve the application exclusively over HTTPS and enable HSTS.
Remove the unauthenticated `public.html` version or protect it with at least basic authentication.
Add proper logout handling that revokes tokens on the identity provider and clears server‑side sessions.
Consider using Microsoft Entra ID and Okta SDKs’ built‑in token validation utilities rather than manual claim checks.
Log authentication and authorization events and monitor for anomalous activity.
